---
title: "Module 0 - Tutorial 1"
subtitle: "Colab + GPU + Tensors"
number-sections: true
date: "2026-01-01"
date-modified: today
date-format: long
categories: ['M0:', 'Tutorial']
description: "Tutorial for Module 0 focused on Colab + GPU + Tensors (Detailed)."
---

# Module Learning Objectives {.unnumbered}

Module 0 builds the computational and mathematical foundation for the rest of AD698. By the end of this module, students should be able to:

1. work reliably in Google Colab and Google Drive
2. inspect and manipulate tensors in PyTorch
3. connect mathematical objects such as vectors, matrices, and tensors to code
4. prepare for later neural-network, NLP, and generative AI workflows

## Tutorial Learning Objectives {.unnumbered}

By the end of this tutorial, you should be able to:

1. Start and validate a GPU runtime.
2. Mount and organize Google Drive for AD698.
3. Build and manipulate tensors of different ranks.
4. Run a simple CPU vs GPU comparison.

## How This Tutorial Connects {.unnumbered}

- `M0_Lab1` expects students to use Colab cleanly and understand runtime behavior.
- `M0_A` depends on basic notebook execution, tensor fluency, and file organization.
- Later modules reuse this workflow for text modeling, embeddings, and generative AI experiments.


# Part 1: Colab Setup

## Step 1: Create notebook and configure runtime

1. Open [Google Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/)
2. Create a new notebook
3. Rename it `AD698_M0T1_<your-bu-id>.ipynb`
4. Set runtime to GPU: `Runtime -> Change runtime type -> T4 GPU`

## Step 2: Verify environment

```{python}
#| eval: false
import platform
import torch

print("Python:", platform.python_version())
print("PyTorch:", torch.__version__)
print("CUDA available:", torch.cuda.is_available())
if torch.cuda.is_available():
    print("GPU:", torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))
```

Expected:

1. PyTorch version shown.
2. CUDA available is `True` for GPU runtime.

:::{.callout-note}
Colab does not guarantee a specific GPU type or uninterrupted runtime length. If GPU is unavailable, verify the runtime first and then continue on CPU for the tensor examples if needed.
:::


# Part 2: Drive and File Hygiene

## Step 3: Mount Drive

```{python}
#| eval: false
from google.colab import drive

drive.mount('/content/drive')
```

## Step 4: Build AD698 folder structure

```{python}
#| eval: false
import os

base = "/content/drive/MyDrive/AD698/M0"
os.makedirs(f"{base}/notebooks", exist_ok=True)
os.makedirs(f"{base}/data", exist_ok=True)
os.makedirs(f"{base}/outputs", exist_ok=True)
print("Folder ready:", base)
```

:::{.callout-warning}
Anything in `/content` can disappear after runtime reset. Save files in Drive-backed directories.
:::

:::{.callout-note}
If `drive.mount()` is slow or times out, one common cause is that `My Drive` or a target folder has too many directly stored items. Keep Drive organized into subfolders.
:::


# Part 3: Tensor Walkthrough

## Example A: rank, shape, dtype

```{python}
#| eval: false
import torch

scalar = torch.tensor(7)
vector = torch.tensor([1, 2, 3])
matrix = torch.arange(12).reshape(3, 4)

print("scalar rank:", scalar.ndim, "shape:", tuple(scalar.shape))
print("vector rank:", vector.ndim, "shape:", tuple(vector.shape))
print("matrix rank:", matrix.ndim, "shape:", tuple(matrix.shape), "dtype:", matrix.dtype)
```

## Example B: create from raw sequence

```{python}
#| eval: false
raw = torch.arange(16)
rank2 = raw.reshape(2, 8)
rank3 = raw.reshape(2, 4, 2)

print("rank2 shape:", rank2.shape)
print(rank2)

print("rank3 shape:", rank3.shape)
print(rank3)
```

## Example C: indexing and slicing

```{python}
#| eval: false
x = torch.arange(20).reshape(4, 5)
print("x:\n", x)
print("row 2:", x[1])
print("col 4:", x[:, 3])
print("block:\n", x[1:3, 2:5])
```

## Example D: broadcasting and reductions

```{python}
#| eval: false
x = torch.arange(12).reshape(3, 4).float()
bias = torch.tensor([0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4])

print("broadcast add:\n", x + bias)
print("sum over rows (dim=1):", x.sum(dim=1))
print("mean over cols (dim=0):", x.mean(dim=0))
```


# Part 4: Move to GPU + Benchmark

## Example E: device transfer

```{python}
#| eval: false
device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
x = torch.arange(12).reshape(3, 4).float()
x_dev = x.to(device)
print("device:", x_dev.device)
```

## Example F: matrix multiply timing

```{python}
#| eval: false
import time

size = 2000
a_cpu = torch.randn(size, size)
b_cpu = torch.randn(size, size)

t0 = time.time()
_ = a_cpu @ b_cpu
cpu_s = time.time() - t0

if torch.cuda.is_available():
    a_gpu = a_cpu.cuda()
    b_gpu = b_cpu.cuda()
    torch.cuda.synchronize()
    t1 = time.time()
    _ = a_gpu @ b_gpu
    torch.cuda.synchronize()
    gpu_s = time.time() - t1
    print(f"CPU: {cpu_s:.4f}s | GPU: {gpu_s:.4f}s")
else:
    print(f"CPU: {cpu_s:.4f}s | GPU not available")
```


# Quick Practice Questions (Tutorial)

Try these directly after the examples:

1. Create a tensor with values 0 to 23 and reshape to shape `(3, 2, 4)`.
2. From that tensor, extract the first 2D block and compute its column sums.
3. Create a `(3, 4)` matrix and a `(4,)` vector. Add them using broadcasting.
4. Move a random `(1000, 1000)` tensor to GPU (if available) and print its device.


# Troubleshooting

## CUDA is false

1. Re-select GPU runtime.
2. Restart runtime.
3. Re-run setup cells.

## Drive issues

1. Re-run mount cell.
2. Confirm correct Google account.
3. Check whether `My Drive` or the target folder contains too many directly stored files.
4. Prefer fewer, larger files over many tiny reads when possible.

## Out-of-memory

1. Reduce tensor sizes.
2. Restart runtime.

## Sharing reminder

If you share a Colab notebook, the code, markdown, and saved outputs may be shared, but the temporary runtime state is not. Include setup cells so another student or instructor can reproduce the tutorial notebook.


# Instructor Internal Reference (Hidden)

The following cells mirror the internal notebook workflow used for preparation.
They are hidden in rendered output.

```{python}
#| eval: false
#| include: false
import platform
import torch

print("Python:", platform.python_version())
print("PyTorch:", torch.__version__)
print("CUDA available:", torch.cuda.is_available())
print("GPU count:", torch.cuda.device_count())
if torch.cuda.is_available():
    print("GPU name:", torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))
```

```{python}
#| eval: false
#| include: false
import os

base = None
try:
    from google.colab import drive
    drive.mount('/content/drive')
    base = '/content/drive/MyDrive/AD698/M0'
    os.makedirs(f'{base}/notebooks', exist_ok=True)
    os.makedirs(f'{base}/data', exist_ok=True)
    os.makedirs(f'{base}/outputs', exist_ok=True)
except ModuleNotFoundError:
    pass
```


# Resources

1. [Google Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/)
2. [PyTorch Tensor Docs](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html)
3. [Tensor Notes Reference](https://notes.101ai.net/basics/tensors)

## AI Use and Share Links {.unnumbered}

If generative AI materially supports your work for this tutorial, include an AI disclosure appendix or separate AI disclosure document if your instructor requests one. Include the complete prompt(s), relevant output excerpt(s), validation steps, and direct shared chat link(s) when available.

![](./M0_lecture01_figures/chatgpt-share.jpg){width="80%" fig-align="center"}

![](./M0_lecture01_figures/gemini-share-conversation.png){width="80%" fig-align="center"}

When possible, use **one continuous chat** for the activity so the full reasoning trail can be reviewed in one place. If you used multiple chats, share **all chats directly related** to the work.

Tools such as **ChatGPT**, **Claude**, and **Gemini** support direct chat sharing. If sharing or export is not available, include copied prompt/output evidence or screenshots instead. Because shared links may be viewable by anyone with the link, do not include confidential, personal, or restricted information in those chats.
